questions.question: Explain the conditions required for binary search to be applicable on a list and provide the recurrence relation for its time complexity in the worst case.
keyPoints: List must be sorted (or sorted after a transformation).
Random access (array or equivalent) is needed.
Binary search divides the problem size by 2 each step.
T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) → O(log n) worst-case time.

questions.question: Illustrate the steps of building a max‑heap from the following list: [12, 5, 20, 8, 3, 15]. Show the state of the array after each heapify operation.
keyPoints: Insert elements sequentially and apply heapify (down‑heap) from the last non‑leaf node.
First heapify at index floor(n/2)-1 = 2 (value 20) – no change.
Heapify at index 1 (value 5) → swap with larger child 15, then heapify subtree.
Heapify at index 0 (value 12) → swap with 20, then heapify, final max‑heap: [20, 12, 15, 5, 3, 8].

questions.question: Write the pseudocode for QuickSort that uses the last element as pivot. Analyze its time complexity for best, average, and worst‑case inputs, and explain how the algorithm behaves on an already sorted array.
keyPoints: Pseudocode includes PARTITION(A, low, high) and QUICKSORT(A, low, high).
PARTITION swaps elements ≤ pivot to the left side and returns pivot final index.
Best/average case: O(n log n) when pivot consistently divides list near middle; recurrence T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n).
Worst case O(n²) when pivot is always smallest/largest (e.g., sorted array with last element pivot); can be mitigated by random pivot or median‑of‑three.

questions.question: Describe the MergeSort algorithm using recursive pseudocode. Derive its time and space complexities. Additionally, explain how MergeSort can be adapted to count the number of inversions in an array and provide the modified pseudocode.
keyPoints: MergeSort recursively splits array into halves, sorts each half, then merges sorted halves (O(n log n) time).
Recurrence: T(n)=2T(n/2)+Θ(n) solves to Θ(n log n); auxiliary space Θ(n) for the temporary array.
To count inversions, during the merge step count pairs (i,j) where left[i] > right[j]; add this count to total.
Modified MERGE function returns both sorted array and inversion count; overall algorithm returns total inversions in O(n log n) time and O(n) extra space.
